speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three for Daniel Geslewitz. You were talking about some of the details of life in the ghetto, and I really would like you to continue to talk about that. I'll tell you what. Why don't you start with-- I'm curious about you were-- you were a teenager in the ghetto. What-- what kinds of-- was there any kind of social life? Did you have friends? What kinds of-- what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the friends were in the factory that you worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was the social life. There was no social life, absolutely. You didn't think about the social. What you think is only about food. When-- when you're hungry, and as hungry as we were, you don't-- you only think about food. You wait for food. You wait for the little bit of soup they're gonna give you, the little bit the water with that-- the-- the piece of-- well-- well, you know, the piece of kohlrabi. A piece of potato will-- will-- so-- will be on top. Or you get a [INAUDIBLE] [? soup. ?] You only wait-- you wait for the food. You-- you got the stomach cramps, and you-- you wait till you get a little bit warm-- as long as it's warm liquid, you could fool your stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never thought about girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never looked at girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I tell you. We-- we human beings. And we-- we have certain, you know, feelings, and you worked together with girls. But we never got involved. I never got involved with girls. Even we work with girls, at the beginning, we work with girls, and I liked a girl. You know, I had-- but I never, never had anything, anything, you know, to-- to-- you know, like personal, absolutely. And but-- but person-- but I liked her as a person, because you worked in the same factory. And-- and I remember-- you know, if I could, I would help her. The only way I could help her is by giving the wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wood was a commodity in the Lodz ghetto. Because I worked in wood, I had-- I had the privilege, or whatever it is, from the cutoffs, little pieces, I could take home. Oh, if she wants to take it home, she get it-- had-- somebody had to give it to her. So me working by the machines and having those-- how do you call it? The-- the waste. The shreds and the-- That's right cutoffs, you know? I would save and give it to her. And that-- that meant a lot. I couldn't-- I couldn't do anything else. So instead of flowers and candy--

speaker: interviewee
text: Forget about flowers and candy. Forget a piece of bread. But you-- you helped her with the wood. But there was nothing personal. But I-- and then when the ghetto was liquidated--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. And-- and my brother came and we were all hiding. We hid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you knew they were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'll get to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we were hiding in 1944, when they were liquidating the ghetto, my brother Leon came and he said, I saw, you know-- her name was [? Yerka. ?] And he said, I saw her walk by. And-- and I went up, and she wasn't there anymore. And I haven't seen her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- the-- you know, there were people that you knew, and all of a sudden disappeared, and you didn't see-- and they didn't see them anymore. That was-- this-- but that was the norm. But was no time to get involved, in the ghetto, with like girls, boys, romances, and all those things. That-- that-- there were a bigger problems than that. OK, you started to tell me, in between tapes, about some other things that went on in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they  went out-- deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead and talk about deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one big deportation in 1942. We couldn't get out. They called a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like a curfew. And they went house to house or whatever. They took out people, out of the ghetto. And they had always had the quota. When-- when the quota was fulfilled, then they stopped, and things went back to normal. I guess in September, in 1942, the Germans declared they're gonna make the ghetto-- instead of a ghetto, a camp,  a working camp, or something like this. They always had those gimmicks or something, reasons. Therefore, all the children have to be taken out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, September. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And another thing, that the Jewish administration had to deliver those children to them. But it was a slow process.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they say children, what ages do they mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them away and put them on trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Age was from two, three, four, five up to 13. There was no thing-- if-- then the SS came in. I'll explain to you. And they went into a, you know, a courtyard. And there was lots of courtyards. And they made everybody come down into the courtyard. The SS men would point out. You go here, or you go here. Was at his whim. And now we see children. If he was, in his-- in his opinion, he was old enough, he made him go one side. He was younger, he was taken to be put in the other side, were taken away. He was taken out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In trucks, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: In trucks. In the process, the Germans-- they was-- the-- the Jewish administration was too slow, so the SS came in. In the process, they used to throw our kids out of the window, right on the truck. They usually smashed their heads against the wall. And-- and they took out-- and then the older people. They took away the older people. And I remember they came-- we tried to hide, you know, to bypass. We knew way to go, and we used to, like, get around. But once they came in. They spotted a woman. And-- and-- and the SS called her, but she went to our courtyard. That made it-- we all had to go down. And I remember, we-- we saved our mother, because he made our mother to go to this side. And we pulled her to our side and pushed her behind the young people. So she stayed with us. You know, she was not taken away. But my father was taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But we managed, him being a-- a craftsman, he would-- we took them out. You know, the-- the factory that he worked at said that he's a very important-- he's a very good craftsman. We managed to take him out. Shortly after that, he died. He never was-- it was terrible. They used to take them to a certain spot, and from there, they used to take them out by trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back, just for a minute, to the time when they took the children away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have-- you had one sister with a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she had one sister with a child. We hid it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you hid it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hid. We hid the child. But once the-- the-- that [NON-ENGLISH], how do you call it-- the-- the curfew was over, and they got their amount of people, they left. They-- they were satisfied. You know what I am-- they were satisfied.

speaker: interviewer
text: The norm was to deliver 20,000 people. 20,000 were taken out of the ghetto. Things went back to normal, so to speak. I mean for us, you know? They didn't bother to come in. Because the Germans didn't come into the ghetto, except for they had to do something. So what happened to your nephew?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hide-- we hid him. And then he-- he-- but he was taken in 1944 to Auschwitz, with the mother. And-- and then, you know, he was gassed with mother-- and my-- and my-- with his mother and my mother. You know, when we were together. I'll-- I'll get to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the children, they took out the children. It was a terrible time. You-- you had to hide. And you forgot about the hunger. Even we are hungry, forgot about the food. But somehow, the-- the family managed to stay together after the [INAUDIBLE]. Except my father died after-- right afterward. He died from hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So by now--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were last two brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my brother is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hersh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersh was gone, and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now in 1944, they liquidated the ghetto. There came an order. They-- they will liquidate the ghetto. And the liquidation started in June. And again, they-- you know, they were always deceiving, the Germans. And they said they gonna move the ghetto, not liquidate. They're going to move from one place to the other, to a different-- to a safer place. But the Russian front is coming close, and they will-- you know, there's going to be fighting. So factories should be moved as a unit. But we didn't believe them. And we didn't-- we didn't, you know, follow their orders. And we hid in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find hiding places? Where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we hid-- any place we could find. We thought this was a place, and-- and we were quite successful. We-- we had in our house, there were-- where the-- the German family lived, they had three rooms. One room-- from this room, you went into the second room. And from this one was a small room, the third room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to work in from one to the other. So the last room, we-- we blocked it off with a cabinet, I put the cabinet and books inside, the door. We camouflaged the door. We used to crawl into the bottom and then put the books back on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- in some instances, you could hear the-- the-- the Germans-- the SS men, walking up the steps and walking into the room. You could hear. They were so quiet. But they didn't detect us. And-- and by the end of-- there were-- there were hardly any Jews left in the ghetto. And-- and there-- there was one guy, a family, a father and son and-- and his mother. We worked together, and they were hiding with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the father said, you want to go out and have a look? For some reason. He went out, and he was spotted by an SS man. So he said, you know, he has a family, they have to come with them. So he said his family wants families to go together. So that's how they discovered us. And-- and they took us-- you know, they came, let's go. They took this away. So that was your whole family then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole-- that-- that was-- that was my brother with his wife. Another wife. He got married. That was the guy who--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was Mayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Leon, that-- who survived. He got married in the ghetto. Now, his wife. [? Shmiel-- ?] that was my older. My-- my sister Lela, right? And my sister Chaya with-- with-- with the boy, and her husband. We were about 13 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mom. And we were taken away to the-- to the-- to the railroad siding. And then we were put in cattle cars. And that was one was first-- that was my first railroad journey in my life. If I looked out the little window, I saw the fields and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we didn't know where they were taking us. And they took us to Auschwitz, I found out later. And we got to Auschwitz early in the morning. And the doors opened, and there was the shouting. You know, that-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, in German. And we managed. One was following after the other, you know? And then we-- we found ourselves on the siding of the railroad, on the platform.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were then divided. They pushed us to the end. And then we saw a-- a German officer, and they made us walk toward the German officer. And the officer-- we didn't have any idea what-- what's going on. And as he walked, he was-- with his finger, he was gesturing. He would show, you go here, you go here, or you go here. And he separated us from my mother and my sister. And me and my brother, my-- I have two brothers-- went to the other side. Mayer wasn't there, because we were not together in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- oh, you weren't with Mayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not hiding. Mayer was not hiding with us. So all the three brothers went to one side. My sister went to the other side, with her-- with her sister-in-law. But my mother, with one sister, with the baby, went to the other side. And then they marched us away. You know, they separated us. And we went-- we were taken to-- to Birkenau, or [? land in ?] Birkenau. And there we were stripped of everything. And we were given-- shaved, you know, the hair and everything. And then we were issued those uniforms, striped uniforms. And the terror started. You know, everything was terror. They-- they start-- they dehumanized us immediately. You know, they had [? helpers, ?] and we wound up in Birkenau. And Birkenau was an awful place. Awful. Awful is not the word. There was-- the-- the terror was everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see your mother or sister again?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as we separated, I never see my mother and my-- and my sister and my nephew again. I found out later that all the people, all the people were taken to the gas chambers. Because Birkenau, if you looked, you could see the crematoriums. You could see the-- the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know. They told me. Other inmates. I asked inmates, because-- you know, I said, how do you get out of here? He said, the only place-- the only-- how you can get out of here, from this place, is through the chimney. That was-- the-- the-- that was-- you know, that's how it was. You know, the only way you can get out of Birkenau is through the chimney. In other words, being gassed and cremated. So I got the picture. But you know, when I was in Birkenau, I hardly looked at those places. I tried to-- I looked at the building, but I didn't want-- I tried not to look at those buildings. So now it's you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Shmiel ?] and Leon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Leon. And I met my-- my brother Mayer in Birkenau. He was there, taken before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the four boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: The four-- four boys, we-- we're still-- we're together. My other sister, with her sister-in-law, was in  the other place. I found out later, after the war. And my mother, with her daughter, with Chaya, and her baby, and her boy, were taken to the gas. I never seen them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now it's the four boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four boys. My sister and her sister-in-law were in Auschwitz, in Birkenau, but the women's camp. But when they liquidated the camp, they marched them all out. And those people who couldn't keep up with the march, they didn't have shoes, they were shot. And that's how I lost my sister and my sister-in-law. Because I knew a witness, somebody else, who-- who told me she was marched out of Auschwitz, too, and she told me what happened. So we were four brothers left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, in-- in August, end of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- being in Birkenau, living under those conditions, we found out that the Germans are registering for work in Germany, for craftsmen. Metalworkers. So I became a metalworker. I went-- we all four registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all register as metalworkers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all registered as metalworkers. We not-- we were not metalworkers. What the heck, you know? There was no such a thing like-- you know, you took chances. What the hell? You know, to get out, you-- you say anything. You would say people lie. We just want to save ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there, and he figured-- he asked, what are you? I told him I'm a klempner. Klempner is a sheet metal worker. So we registered-- registered all four-- all four of us. And we-- and they took us out, but nine days later, they asked us to report to a certain place, and they took us out of Birkenau. And where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took-- went to Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Better spell for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-- B-R-O-N-S-H-W-E-I-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to Braunschweig. We got to Braunschweig, and from there, we stayed in one camp, a small camp. And they took us to Vechelde.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-O-C-H-E-L-D-E. And Vechelde was a factory, a metal-working, a-- They were building parts for trucks. It was a big building. And there they're-- we're assigned to a German worker we called a [? masters, ?] you know? And he would show you what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- me and my brother were assigned to a guy, to a German, and we had a big drill press, and we're supposed to work. He showed us. Well, I was no-- we learned fast. It was an automatic machine, and we learned fast. And in the process, I almost lost three fingers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we stayed, in this place. We were not-- in the beginning, we were physically abused by the kapo-- by the [GERMAN] They were the German criminals. But the worst enemy there was the cold and the hunger. There was no defense. You know, it was a constant hunger. We'd get that little piece of bread, and the soup, and that was it. And people were dying constantly. It was terrible cold. And in this camp, I lost my brother. [? Shmiel ?] died in this camp. He died-- he got sick, and a day later, I came in, he was gone. [? Shmiel ?] died. Mayer got very skinny. He-- it-- it got-- it was like a process. You-- you-- you-- in other words, you lived. You-- somehow, you managed. But all of a sudden, it hit certain people. It-- it-- it was the end. You know, it was the end of the road. And Mayer-- so-- with Mayer, it hit the end of the road. He was nothing but bones-- skin and bones. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll get to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll get to it a minute. When they said that they're gonna liquidate this-- this-- this camp, and they will take us to Braunschweig, my-- we lived-- let me explain to you. We lived with the Germans in the same quarters. Not same quarters. The Germans lived to the side, the SS, and we had to the big  hall. We all slept in one big hall. But they had a kitchen, their own kitchen, the Germans. The German, the SS. And one German SS man put out soup to cool off. Mayer was standing, looking at the soup. He didn't take it. So the German asked him whether he would like to have the soup. You know, he said, yes. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took a whole bunch of salt and put it in the soup, and he made him eat it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mayer got delirious. He didn't know what was going on after.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were deported. Next day, they deported us. They made us-- we walked from Vechelde to Braunschweig in the winter. But Mayer couldn't walk, so they put him on a truck. But we got there. Next day, Mayer died. That's why I showed you the pictures, you know, that he's buried in Watenstedt. You know, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell Watenstedt for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-T-E-N-S-T-A-D-T. I hope it's right. So Mayer-- we got to Watenstedt. From-- from there, Mayer died in Watenstedt. And there were two left.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Leon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Leon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in Watenstedt. We were taken from Watenstedt. We were taken by train. We were supposed to go-- I don't know where. They just put us on trains, and we-- and were on a train for three, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a cattle train, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: All cattle trains. Very limited-- very little food, no food. And always in process, people were dying. You know, sitting in the train. And they always squashed us in, a whole bunch of people. And you had to watch out. We always kept as a group, you know, the Jews from our town, because we tried to stay together. It was-- besides the hunger, we had to watch out for other prisoners, that they were paid by the Germans to finish us off, in certain cases. So as we were-- as a group, we were safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us on the train, and they took us-- we landed in Ravensbruck. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We landed in Ravensbruck. In Ravensbruck, it was the same story as-- Can you spell Ravensbruck for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ravensbruck? R-A-V-E-N-B-R-U-C-K. Ravensbruck, I guess. We landed in Ravensbruck. In Ravensbruck, conditions were the same as in the other camps. Always the hunger present. And then we went through a period where we didn't have water for three days. I don't know what-- what the reason was, but we didn't have water for three days. Finally, after the third day, they gave us water. Ravensbruck, we got packages from the Red Cross. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was written in Yiddish, and then German and in English. But the food that we got in those packages were no use for us. They were more like-- did more bad than good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they send?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send cheese. We hadn't eaten cheese in six years. It's fat. They sent us coffee. But we didn't have pots. We didn't have stoves, right? They send us certain stuff we-- we-- we had to prepare. We didn't have. So you ate it, and instead of benefiting from that, it was a deterrent. Lots of people died from it. My-- my brother ate some dried-- I don't know what. But the food that you-- that was there, you had to soak in water. But you ate a lot, and it swelled up in your stomach. He got very sick. He almost died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the help that we got was-- didn't help us much. And you had to watch it. You had to take care of it. So anyway, the gesture was good, but was-- it wasn't helpful. And from Ravensbruck, they took us again, put us on the train. And they took us to Vechelde. Spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- no, wait. Wobbelin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wobbelin. I mixed up. Wobbelin. W, O-- umlaut-- B-E-L-E-N. And I have a document here to show to you. We came to Wobbelin. That was not far from Ludwigslust. And there, things got real bad. The camp didn't have any floors, no windows. It was just four walls. We were exposed to the elements. There, I got sick. I got the diarrhea. And I could hardly walk anymore. And May the 1st, they put us on the train again. And we stayed in the train, but the train didn't move. May the 2nd, they let us out, but they didn't count us anymore. They usually used to count us. Every time you got out of the train, they used to count us, and then when we went into a train, they used to count us. Nobody should be missing. But this particular day, they didn't count us. And around noontime, I-- I was very sick. I could hardly get up. We knew something is happening, but we didn't know what. And then they came, they said that-- that we-- the French soldiers came to the wires-- they said French soldiers-- and we are liberated. I didn't-- I didn't have the strength to get up and look. I was such a bad-- bad shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was-- where was Leon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And still sick also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon was in better shape. That was good for me. Leon was in better shape. And we were liberated, but I didn't know whether we were liberated by the French. But they say we were liberated by the Americans. It doesn't matter, because we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 2nd, around noontime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told, on the-- on the-- you know, how do you call it? Not officially. One prisoner to the other-- that we shouldn't leave the camp. The camp was open. The doors were open. We could walk out. That the American were going to send in ambulances and doctors, and-- and they-- and they'll help us. So we should not leave the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm gonna stop the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're about at the end, and then we'll go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then we'll finish on the next tape.
